coredata.dc:title: Impedance measurement of lithium-ion batteries using optimized pulse patterns
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lignell, Leevi
Tran, Minh
Roinila, Tomi
coredata.dc:description: As the demand for Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries has surged in recent years, concerns about their environmental impact have grown. Instead of disposing of these batteries in landfills at the end of their lifecycle, interest in recycling cells for second-life applications has increased. A key factor in assessing the reusability of these cells is determining their state of health (SOH). Battery internal impedance, which is closely tied to SOH, serves as a reliable and rapid indicator of a battery’s health status. Recent studies have introduced fast and easily implementable impedance measurement methods based on broadband excitations and Fourier techniques. These methods involve subjecting the battery under test to charge and discharge cycles according to the excitation signal. The resulting voltage and current waveforms are measured, and impedance information is extracted via the Fourier transform. While effective, many broadband excitations are designed as discrete-time sequences, which restrict and limit signal properties in practical applications. This paper proposes a continuous-time excitation design method based on optimized pulse patterns (OPP). The OPP approach identifies optimal time instants for a predefined number of signal level changes within a signal period. Compared to discrete-time signal designs, the OPP method offers several significant advantages. It allows unrestricted signal generation frequencies and can be optimized for any injection device. Additionally, the spectrum shape of the OPP excitation can be largely controlled, enabling maximized perturbation energy at desired frequencies. Experimental measurements using broadband excitations designed with the proposed OPP method demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Impedance measurement
Fourier methods
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Optimized pulse patterns

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Perturbation signals in battery impedance measurements

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the measurement of the impedance of a lithium-ion battery cell.
Impedance of a Li-ion battery as a Nyquist plot.
Equivalent circuit model of lithium-ion battery impedance.
The first half of an OPP sequence with three switching angles per a quarter-cycle. The three switching angles and their counterparts in the second quarter are marked.
Time-domain shapes of the OPP sequences used in the experiments.
Nyqvist plots of the impedance measured using the OPP M300, the OPP M375 and the PRBS with injection amplitudes of 25 mA and 250 mA at 50% SOC.
Nyqvist plots of the impedance measured using the OPP M300, the OPP M375 and the PRBS with injection amplitudes of 25 mA and 250 mA at 10% SOC.
The parameters of OPP sequences used in the experiments.
Error measures of the measurement results obtained at battery SOC 50% and ambient temperature of 25 °C using the OPP signals and the PRBS using 25 mA injection amplitude.
Error measures of the measurement results obtained at battery SOC 50% and ambient temperature of 25 °C using the OPP signals and the PRBS using 250 mA injection amplitude.
Error measures of the measurement results obtained at battery SOC 10% and ambient temperature of 5 °C using the OPP signals and the PRBS using 25 mA injection amplitude.
Error measures of the measurement results obtained at battery SOC 10% and ambient temperature of 5 °C using the OPP signals and the PRBS using 250 mA injection amplitude.

head.ce:title: Impedance measurement of lithium-ion batteries using optimized pulse patterns

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Tampere University, addressline=Kalevantie 4, city=Tampere, postcode=33014, state=, country=Finland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As the demand for Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries has surged in recent years, concerns about their environmental impact have grown. Instead of disposing of these batteries in landfills at the end of their lifecycle, interest in recycling cells for second-life applications has increased. A key factor in assessing the reusability of these cells is determining their state of health (SOH). Battery internal impedance, which is closely tied to SOH, serves as a reliable and rapid indicator of a battery’s health status. Recent studies have introduced fast and easily implementable impedance measurement methods based on broadband excitations and Fourier techniques. These methods involve subjecting the battery under test to charge and discharge cycles according to the excitation signal. The resulting voltage and current waveforms are measured, and impedance information is extracted via the Fourier transform. While effective, many broadband excitations are designed as discrete-time sequences, which restrict and limit signal properties in practical applications. This paper proposes a continuous-time excitation design method based on optimized pulse patterns (OPP). The OPP approach identifies optimal time instants for a predefined number of signal level changes within a signal period. Compared to discrete-time signal designs, the OPP method offers several significant advantages. It allows unrestricted signal generation frequencies and can be optimized for any injection device. Additionally, the spectrum shape of the OPP excitation can be largely controlled, enabling maximized perturbation energy at desired frequencies. Experimental measurements using broadband excitations designed with the proposed OPP method demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Li-ion batteries have emerged as strong candidates for electricity storage solutions. Their high energy density and efficiency, among other properties, make them the preferred choice for energy storage in various applications, such as electric vehicles. While the use of Li-ion batteries does not produce greenhouse gases or other harmful emissions, other environmental aspects must be considered [1]. Li-ion batteries contain various toxic materials and pose a potential environmental hazard if improper waste management practices are applied [2]. One potential solution is to repurpose end-of-life batteries in second-life applications that are less demanding in terms of storage capacity. For example, a used Li-ion battery pack from an electric vehicle can be integrated into a grid-connected, large-scale battery system [3].

Information about the state of a battery cell, particularly the decline in battery capacity, is crucial for evaluating its usability. This decline is most commonly measured by the battery’s state-of-health (SOH), which indicates the remaining maximum capacity compared to the battery cell’s original capacity [4]. Another important parameter is the state-of-charge (SOC), which provides information about the remaining charge in the battery and helps define the battery’s overall quality. Since these parameters are difficult to measure directly, estimation methods based on available data—such as current, voltage, and temperature—have been developed [5]. Many of these state estimation methods rely on computationally complex algorithms or require a substantial amount of data, making their implementation in low-cost systems challenging [6].

A promising method for extracting information about the state of a lithium-ion battery is through the measurement of its internal impedance [7]. The frequency-dependent internal impedance reflects the battery’s electrical dynamic properties, which are strongly correlated with the battery’s SOC, SOH, temperature, and available power [8]. In particular, impedance analysis reveals specific aging mechanisms, offering comprehensive insights into battery health and condition that can enhance the performance of model-based and data-driven state estimation algorithms [9–11]. Battery impedance measurements provide nearly real time information about the battery state and integrating on-board impedance measurements to complement battery management systems (BMS) has been the topic of recent studies [12–14]. Battery impedance has also gained interest in SOH analysis of second-life battery cells that have usually undergone complex degradation processes [15].

The internal impedance of a battery is conventionally measured using sine-sweep-based electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS); however, this method is not suited for practical applications due to its complexity and long measurement time [16]. Recent studies have introduced methods based on broadband perturbations and Fourier techniques to measure battery impedance [16–21]. In these methods, the battery is charged and discharged according to a specific broadband perturbation, and Fourier techniques are applied to the measured voltage and current to obtain the battery impedance. The broadband perturbation contains energy at multiple frequencies, which significantly reduces measurement time compared to conventional EIS. Moreover, the broadband perturbation can be designed with only two or three signal levels, allowing for implementation in low-cost applications where the output can handle only a limited number of signal levels. Examples of binary and near-binary broadband perturbations applied in battery impedance measurements include square-wave perturbation [17,18], pseudo-random binary sequence (PRBS) [16], discrete-interval binary sequence (DIBS) [19], inverse-repeat binary sequence (IRBS) [20], and direct-synthesis ternary sequence (DSTS) [21].

Most of the previously applied binary and near-binary broadband perturbations in battery-impedance measurements are based on discrete-time design. In these cases, the signals are first designed and generated as discrete signals, then converted to continuous-time signals using methods such as zero-order-hold (ZOH) implementation. However, the physical implementation of these discrete-time signals often affects their properties and imposes restrictions and limitations on practical measurements. For example, applying ZOH reduces the perturbation bandwidth and significantly impacts the phase behavior of the estimated impedance, leading to inaccurate information about the system dynamics [16,22]. These effects can be mitigated by designing the binary or near-binary broadband perturbation directly in continuous time.

This paper proposes an excitation design method based on optimized pulse patterns (OPP) [23,24]. In this method, a number of signal levels and transitions between these levels within a signal period are defined, with transitions strategically placed to maximize signal energy at specific harmonic frequencies. The signal is generated with an optimization algorithm that utilizes continuous-time Fourier series to determine the energy of harmonic frequencies of the signal. The OPP signal design method offers significant advantages compared to conventional discrete-time perturbation design. Because the OPP signal is designed directly in continuous time, it retains all of its frequency-domain characteristics, enabling predictable measurements regardless of the injection system’s properties. The flexibility to choose the number of transitions between the signal levels within a signal period allows for efficient concentration of harmonic frequency energy within a specific frequency range, and optimizing the signal generation frequency for the injection device properties.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the measurement principle of battery impedance and discusses its importance in the dynamic analysis of the battery. Section 3 reviews the OPP design method and Section 4 presents experimental measurements using the proposed OPP signals on a commercial Li-ion battery. Finally, Section 5 concludes the paper.

The internal impedance of a lithium-ion battery can be measured using multiple methods [25]. Fig. 1 depicts a battery impedance measurement method based on current perturbation. In this method, a current source is used to inject a perturbation into the battery cell and the voltage at the battery terminals and the injection current are measured. The sampled signals are then averaged over multiple injection periods, and the discrete Fourier transform is applied to obtain the internal impedance
Z
( ω )
as
(1)
Z
( ω )
=
V
( ω )
I
( ω )
where
V
( ω )
and
I
( ω )
denote the Fourier-transformed voltage and current, respectively [16].

One of the most common ways to present the impedance measurement results is by using a Nyquist plot [16]. The Nyquist plot helps visualize the effects of different physical components and chemical processes on the internal impedance of the battery [26]. An example of a Nyquist plot for a Li-ion battery is shown in Fig. 2.

As shown in Fig. 2, different chemical and physical processes dominate different frequency regions of battery impedance. The impedance in the lowest frequency region is primarily affected by Li-ion diffusion in the active material and near the electrode interface. The middle-frequency region, ranging from several hundred mHz to a few kHz, is dominated by ion movement between the electrodes (charge-transfer region) and through the solid-electrolyte interface (SEI). The highest frequencies are dominated by inductive behavior caused by electron movement in the wires and metallic elements of the battery. Similar structure of the battery impedance is found in multiple battery types including nickel manganese cobalt (NMC), lithium-titanite oxide (LTO), and lithium iron phosphate (LFP) [11,19,26–28]. With knowledge of the effects of chemical and physical processes inside the battery cell, the internal impedance of a Li-ion battery can be used to assess changes in the battery states such as the SOC, SOH, or temperature. The frequencies where the impedance reaches a threshold between two process regions are especially interesting regarding state estimation [29]. Such frequencies, also called process frequencies, include the frequency
f
R
where the reactance reaches zero and the impedance is purely ohmic, and the frequency
f
diff
that lies at the boundary of the charge-transfer and the diffusion region as seen in Fig. 3 [29]. These frequencies are sought for the analysis of the internal impedance of multiple Li-ion battery types, and should be included in the impedance measurement bandwidth [30].

Battery impedance measurements are commonly analyzed by finding an equivalent circuit model (ECM) to represent the impedance results. The ECM typically consists of three components: the series resistance
R
series
, the series inductance
L
series
and the low- to mid-frequency region impedance component in series. The structure of the low- to mid-frequency region impedance component varies, but generally, it consists of components with capacitive properties. Fig. 3 shows one of the most commonly used ECMs [31]. In addition to the series resistance and inductance components, the ECM includes the parallel-connected SEI-layer resistance
R
SEI
and SEI-layer capacitance
C
SEI
, the parallel-connected charge-transfer resistance
R
CT
and charge-transfer capacitance
C
CT
, and a Warburg impedance element
W
diffusion
which describes the dynamics of the diffusion region.

To improve the accuracy of the ECM, linear RC-elements are sometimes replaced by ZARC-elements where the capacitor is replaced by a non-linear constant-phase element (CPE) [31]. The parameter values of the components used in the ECM can then serve as a basis for state estimation methods [32]. For example, studies have shown the development of the values of
R
series
and
L
series
to be almost linearly dependent on the battery SOH [33,34]. In contrast, the parameters of the capacitive elements are more closely related to the SOC and cell temperature [33].

The selection of a proper ECM for state analysis is not straightforward as multiple possible presentations for a single impedance measurement exist [35,36]. The ECM should be able to model the impedance accurately enough with lowest possible complexity for a given state estimation task [35]. It should also be noted that increasing the amount of components in an ECM does not guarantee a higher level of accuracy for the model [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Perturbation signals in battery impedance measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The selection of a perturbation sequence plays a key role in applying the battery-impedance measurement method illustrated in Fig. 1. Recent studies have highlighted the effects of various broadband binary and near-binary perturbations on battery-impedance measurements. The strengths of the broadband measurement methods include fast measurement time, and simple time-domain characteristics that allow the perturbations to be injected with methods suitable for on-line measurements [25]. A commonly used excitation example is the pseudo-random binary sequence (PRBS). PRBS is a deterministic broadband signal that enables rapid measurement of battery impedance across a wide frequency band [16]. However, a limitation of PRBS is its suitability exclusively for linear systems. Non-linear distortions are present in Li-ion batteries and they are mainly caused by redox reactions and double-layer capacitances [37]. Non-linear distortions in Li-ion batteries cause the voltage response to current perturbations to leak energy to higher harmonics of the perturbation frequency [37]. To mitigate the effects of nonlinearities present in battery systems, broadband excitations designed for non-linear systems can be used. These excitations have harmonic spectra where harmonics of certain multiples of the base harmonic contain zero energy [38]. In Li-ion battery impedance measurements, the two-level inverse-repeat binary sequence (IRBS) and the three-level the direct-synthesis ternary sequence (DSTS) have been applied to mitigate non-linear behavior [20,21]. The perturbations contain zero energy in the harmonics that are multiple of two, and multiples of two and three, respectively [38]. Regardless, the perturbation amplitude should be kept as low as possible to mitigate the effects of non-linear distortions to the impedance measurements [37]. To enhance the signal-to-noise ratio in measurements while retaining a low time-domain perturbation amplitude, the discrete-interval binary sequence (DIBS) has been proposed [19]. However, this sequence does not address non-linearities, and the energy of the highest harmonic frequencies diminishes when injected into a physical system.

Most of the previously presented broadband techniques have been based on discrete-time design. However, the bandwidth of excitations designed as discrete-time sequences is limited to approximately half of the signal generation frequency when injected into a physical system [16]. In practice, the measurement bandwidth is constrained to much smaller values due to the limitations of the injection equipment [19]. Moreover, the bandwidth limitations of the injection devices can lead to further complications. For example, the DSTS contains multiple sharp transitions, which can be challenging for many generation devices. These sharp transitions also complicate signal measurement, particularly if the measurement device’s sampling frequency experiences even a small amount of jitter [39]. Determining the impedance measurement bandwidth is further complicated because the length of most sequences designed in discrete time is largely uncontrolled. For example, the PRBS can only take lengths defined by
2
n
− 1
where n is an integer, and similarly, a quadratic residue binary sequence must have a length equal to a prime number. More specialized sequences, designed for nonlinear system identification and often used in lithium-ion battery impedance measurements, are typically based on these types of sequences [38]. The fixed length of many sequences can lead to compromises in the measurement; using a sequence that is too long will extend the measurement time, while raising the generation frequency can decrease measurement accuracy.

Optimized pulse patterns (OPP) are pulse-width modulation (PWM) sequences designed to eliminate undesired harmonics from the signal waveform [23]. An OPP sequence consists of a set of M switching angles,
α
i
, that remove specific frequency harmonics from the signal. In practice, these switching angles are determined as a numerical solution to a complex optimization problem involving the harmonic amplitudes in the Fourier series of the sequence [24]. By modifying the objective function of this optimization problem, the OPP method can be adapted to generate a signal suitable for broadband impedance measurements. Although OPP sequences have typically been applied to converters with multiple output levels, this paper utilizes a two-level OPP introduced in [23].

To generate an OPP sequence, an objective function linking the M switching angles
α
i
and the energy of the harmonic frequencies of the sequence must be produced. To construct the objective function, the OPP sequence must be presented as a function. The function is defined as piecewise continuous and having a constant value of either −1 or 1 between two switching angles
α
i
and
α
i + 1
. Because the OPP is designed to be half-wave symmetric, only half a period of the sequence must be defined [23].

A half-period in an OPP sequence will contain 2 M signal level changes. The signal level changes are arranged in a way that produces quarter-wave symmetry in the sequence. An example of the placement of the switching angles
α
i
is shown in Fig. 4. In the figure, the first half-period of an OPP sequence with
M = 3
signal level changes within a quarter-period is shown. As the figure indicates, every switching angle
α
i
has a complementary switching angle
18
0
∘
−
α
2 M − i
with an inverted signal level change.

To define the function
f
( ω t )
fully within a half-period, two additional switching angles are introduced:
α
0
=
0
∘
and
α
2 M + 1
= 18
0
∘
. With these design parameters, the OPP sequence
f
( ω t )
can now be written as:
(2)
f
( ω t )
=
− 1
,
α
2 k
< ω t <
α

1

α
2 k + 1
< ω t <
α
2
( k + 1 )
where k is an integer including zero.

To place the switching angles
α
i
so that the energy of the OPP sequence harmonics is maximized, the Fourier series of
f
( ω t )
must be formed. The Fourier series consists of two coefficients,
a
n
and
b
n
, which represent the amplitudes of the sine and cosine components, respectively, of the n th harmonic of the OPP sequence. The formulas for the Fourier coefficients of the OPP sequence
f
( ω t )
are derived in [23] as follows:
(3)

n

The calculation of the coefficients and the formation of the optimization function are greatly simplified by the quarter-wave symmetry properties. Quarter-wave symmetry property for a function
g
( t )
with a period of T is defined as
(5)

[23]. First, the coefficients
b
n
are zero for every n due to quarter-wave symmetry. Second, the coefficients
a
n
are zero for every even harmonic n
[24].

The computation of the remaining odd-order coefficients
a
n
can be further simplified using half-wave symmetry. Half-wave symmetry for a signal
g
( t )
with a period of T is defined as
(6)

2

[23]. From half-wave symmetry it also follows that all even-order non-linearities of the battery-impedance are suppressed as the energy of all even harmonics is zero [22].

As both
f
( ω t )
and sin(
n ω t
) are half-wave symmetric relative to the same period, and based on (6), the odd-order coefficients can now be written as:
(7)

Using the definition (2), (7) can now be presented as
(8)

To further simplify (8), the quarter-wave symmetry property (5) of the OPP sequence and the cosine function are utilized. From quarter-wave symmetry, it follows that:
(9)

α
2 M + 1 − i
Applying (9) to the cosine functions in (8) yields
(10)

i

( n

( n

for all odd n . Finally, substituting (10) to the terms
M < i ≤ 2 M + 1
in (8) yields
(11)

4

.
The resulting formula for
a
n
contains only M switching angles in the first quadrant of the sequence, with the term
α
0
having been canceled out. Using (11), the amplitude of the n th harmonic of the OPP sequence,
u
n
, can be written as:
(12)
u

a

2
.
Equation (12) is used to develop the objective function J for the optimization. Substituting (10) to (12) gives
(13)
u

2

For the objective function J , the square root and the constant term
4 / π
are omitted. To emphasize the amplitude of higher-order harmonics, the formula is multiplied by a weighing term
n
2
. With these modifications, the objective function becomes suitable for a maximization problem. However, for some solvers, it is more appropriate to formulate the optimization problem as a minimization. Therefore, the complement of the simplified form of (13) is chosen as the objective function. This form is preferred because a reciprocal form can easily produce trivial solutions when the value of J approaches zero during the search for the solution. The objective function
J
(
α
i
)
can now be written as:
(14)
J
(
α
i
)
=
∑
n = 3 , 5 , 7 , …
− n ∗
[
− 1 + 2
∑
i = 1
M
( − 1 )
i + 1
cos
( n
α
i
)
]
2
The optimization problem can now be written as:
(15)
min
α
i
J
(
α
i
)
subject to
0 ≤
α
1
≤
α
2
≤ ⋯ ≤
α
M
≤ 9
0
∘
Solving the optimization problem in (15) provides the switching angles
α
i
for the OPP signal. The optimization problems in this work are solved with Matlab’s fmincon-function. Examples of two OPP signals with a one-second period are presented in the time domain and the frequency domain in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6, respectively. The signals are designed to contain
M = 38
and
M = 81
changes in excitation level within a quarter-cycle.

When designing an OPP sequence, the harmonic properties of PWM can used to determine the number of switching angles M . The frequency spectrum of a PWM signal contains strong sidebands at integer multiples of the switching frequency, so the choice of M should be made so that the sidebands occur in frequency ranges of interest [40].

As M is the amount of changes of level in one quarter-cycle, the total amount of level changes in a full cycle is
4 M
. Given the fundamental frequency
f
1
, the average switching frequency has a value
(16)
f
sw
= 4 M ∗
f
1
To enhance the performance of the injection device, the value of average switching frequency should not exceed the injection device bandwidth.

The cost function J can also be written in other forms than (14). To generate an OPP signal with properties that better suit the measurement purpose, the cost function J can be modified in multiple ways. One method is to use a different weighing factor than
n
2
to shift the frequencies where the energy is concentrated. For example, an OPP with
M = 50
signal level changes within a quarter-period, replacing the weighing factor
n
2
by
− n
( n − 74 )
can be used to emphasize the energy of the frequencies around the 30th harmonic. Another method is to specify the harmonics n that are included in the cost function or to add a penalizing term to specify some frequencies where little energy is wished to be concentrated. Such frequencies might include possible resonance frequencies of the converter system connected to the battery. Consideration of the converter dynamics helps optimize the performance of the injection device by enhancing the converter system stability [41]. As a more systematic approach regarding device limitations, placing a root of the weighing factor on the injection device bandwidth can be used as a starting point for creating a polynomial weighing factor. With this approach, the generated OPP signal will have energy concentrated on user specified frequencies.

All measurements were conducted using a BAK 21700 NMC Li-ion battery cell. The measurement schematic is shown in Fig. 7. The perturbations were generated by a PC and sent to the data-acquisition unit (Nidaq USB-6363) for injection through the bi-directional power supply (Kepco BOP 50-20MG, with a control bandwidth of up to 1 kHz). Voltage and current measurements were performed using the four-wire method, with current measurements taken by Tektronix TCP312 A. The Hall effect sensor was paired with a Tektronix TCPA300 amplifier.

The battery cell under test is placed in a temperature chamber, which also acts as a Faraday cage. Measurement data is transmitted via coaxial cables to further minimize electromagnetic interference (EMI). The measurement software is implemented using the Julia programming language [42]. The measurement setup is shown in Fig. 8.
Two sets of experiments were performed on the battery cell, with the following measurement conditions established for each experiment:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Linear operating conditions The cell was discharged to 50% SOC and soaked in a temperature chamber at 25 °C . In this environment, the cell voltage was stable during current perturbations, and the temperature was high enough for non-linearities to have marginal effect on the measurement result [43,44].

Non-linear operating conditions The cell was discharged to 10% SOC and the measurement temperature was set to 5 °C . At temperatures below 10 °C and low SOC, battery impedance was strongly affected by non-linearities. This second measurement represents impedance conditions far from optimal [43].

ce:section.ce:para: The SOC of the battery cell was estimated using Coulomb counting. In both measurement conditions, the following measurements are performed:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The battery-impedance was measured using a sinusoidal perturbation with an injection amplitude of 250 mA, equivalent to a 0.05C rate. The impedance was measured at 50 logarithmically spaced frequencies. This measurement serves as a reference.
The battery impedance was measured using OPP signals and a PRBS with an injection amplitude of 25 mA (0.005C rate).
The battery impedance was measured using OPP signals and a PRBS with an injection amplitude of 250 mA (0.05C rate).

ce:section.ce:para: The bandwidth of the impedance measurement was selected to range from 0.75 Hz to 1000 Hz. At 50% SOC, the lowest frequencies within this range correspond to the diffusion region, while the highest frequencies were in the ohmic and inductive regions, thus covering the entire charge-transfer and SEI regions of the battery impedance.

Three OPP sequences are used in the experiments, with 300 and 375 changes in level per quarter cycle. These sequences are labeled OPP M300 and OPP M375, respectively. The signals were generated using the cost function (14) and the frequencies specified for use in the analysis are those with at least 20% of the energy of the base harmonic. The threshold value of 20% was selected as the measurements are performed in laboratory conditions with low noise. In measurement conditions where great amount of EMI or other external sources of noise are present, the threshold value can be raised to achieve a higher signal-to-noise ratio. For the injection, the switching angles of the OPP sequences were rounded to the nearest 0.1°. The parameters of the OPP signals and the PRBS used in the experiments are presented in Table 1 and a part of the excitations are presented in the time-domain in Fig. 9.

Eleven periods of each excitation were injected into the battery. The first complete excitation sequence was discarded due to transient response, and the remaining ten periods were averaged to calculate the impedance.

To quantitatively compare the accuracy of the measurement results, the normalized mean absolute error (NMAE) and normalized root mean square error (NRMSE) were evaluated for the broadband excitations. The NMAE is computed as
(17)
e
nmae
=
1
k
∑
j = 1
k
|
Z
reference
( j )
−
Z
broadband
( j )
|
|
Z
reference,max
|
−
|
Z
reference,min
|
where k is the amount of samples,
Z
reference
( j )
is the impedance obtained in the reference measurement,
|
Z
reference,max
|
and
|
Z
reference,min
|
are the maximum and minimum impedance magnitudes of the reference and
Z
broadband
( j )
is the impedance value obtained with the broadband excitation. The same values can be used to calculate the NRMSE as:
(18)
e
nrmse
=
∑
j = 1
k
1
k
∗
(
Z
reference
( j )
−
Z
broadband
( j )
)
2
|
Z
reference,max
|
−
|
Z
reference,min
|
Using both error measures can be useful when the distribution of the measurement error is unknown [45]. The NRMSE is more sensitive to large magnitude errors than the NMAE, and it can therefore be used to assess the possible effects of the sample size and large individual errors on the NMAE value [46].

Conducting a reference measurement at each of the specified frequencies of the OPP signals is not practical; therefore, the reference measurements were restricted to 50 logarithmically spaced frequencies. To find a reference value for each harmonic of the OPP signals, the impedance reference values between two frequencies of the reference measurement were linearly interpolated. Linear interpolation was chosen in favor of an ECM due to the ambiguity of selecting a proper ECM for reference.

The error measures were calculated for three different frequency regions, each spanning approximately one decade from the base frequency of 0.75 Hz. The approximate limits of the frequency regions are 0.75 Hz–7.5 Hz, 7.5 Hz–75 Hz and 75 Hz–750 Hz. Higher frequencies are shown in the figures but are not included in the error analysis due to the bandwidth limitations of the power supply.

It should be noted that the reference measurement frequencies are logarithmically spaced, while the frequencies selected for analysis from the OPP signals and the PRBS are more densely distributed. As a result, the linear interpolation of the reference values may introduce more significant errors at higher frequencies than at lower frequencies. However, this sparse placement of the reference measurement frequencies was used because the base harmonic and the frequency resolution of the PRBS and the OPP signals were different.

The first set of measurements was conducted at 50% battery SOC and a temperature of 25 °C. Fig. 10 shows the impedance measured with both injection amplitudes using the OPP M300, OPP M375 and the PRBS as Nyquist plots.

Fig. 10 shows that the semi-circle shape seen in the impedance reference Nyquist plot is not visible in any of the measurement results obtained using the 25 mA injection amplitude. However, with the 250 mA injection amplitude, the semi-circle shape is clearly visible, though some spread of the plot points is observed, especially at the top of the semi-circle.

The error measures of the measurement results obtained with the OPP signals and the PRBS are presented in Table 2 for the 25 mA injection amplitude and in Table 3 for the 250 mA injection amplitude. Comparing the values in Table 3 to those in Table 2 shows that the increased injection amplitude drastically reduces the error measures, especially in the highest frequency range. The high NRMSE values in Table 2 at the highest frequency range indicate large-magnitude errors.

The second set of measurements was conducted under more challenging conditions, with the battery at 10% SOC and an ambient temperature of 5 °C. The measurement results using the two OPP signals and the PRBS with injection amplitudes of 25 mA and 250 mA are presented in Fig. 11.

The error measures of the impedance measurement results obtained using the OPP signals and the PRBS are presented in Table 4 for the 25 mA injection amplitude and in Table 5 for the 250 mA injection amplitude.

The error measure values in Table 4 for the lower and middle frequency regions are comparable to those in Table 3. However, at the highest frequencies, the error measure values in Table 4 are considerably higher than those in Table 3. Also, the PRBS has the lowest error measure values in the high frequency region even though the Nyquist plot of the measurement results spread around the reference more than the Nyquist plots of the OPP signal results. This is most likely due to some measurement points deviating significantly more from the reference than average as also indicated by the high NRMSE values compared to the NMAE values of both OPP signals. The errors in the high frequency region can be mainly attributed to measurement noise. The outlier points in the high-frequency region of the OPP M300 signal are caused by the harmonic selection criteria used in the analysis. Specifically, a harmonic was included if its energy was at least 20% of the energy of the first harmonic of the OPP signal. As a result, some harmonics with relatively low energy were included in the analysis. In comparison, the energy of PRBS harmonics around 750 Hz was still approximately 90% of that of the first harmonic. By raising the threshold for harmonic energy of the OPP, the magnitude of such errors can be reduced.

The very low values of both NMAE and NRMSE for OPP M300 in Table 5 suggest that this sequence provides accurate impedance measurement results across a wide measurement bandwidth. Comparing the values in Table 5 with those in Table 3 shows that the measurement results obtained at 10% SOC and 5 °C deviate less from the reference than those obtained under conditions where the battery SOC was 50% and the temperature was 25 °C.

The experimental results presented above are contrary to what was expected. The second set of experiments, conducted with the battery at 10% SOC and an ambient temperature of 5 °C, was expected to be more challenging for measuring internal impedance. However, the results indicate that the OPP signals provided more accurate measurements under these lower temperature and SOC conditions than in the first set of measurements, where both the temperature and SOC were higher. The deviation from expected behavior can be explained by the measurement setup and the incremental capacity of the battery. In all the measurements, the same resolution was used by the digital-to-analog (DA) converter of the data-acquisition device. Because the battery impedance in the second measurement set was up to three times higher than in the first set, the voltage perturbation caused by the current perturbation was also larger, reducing the rounding error of the DA conversion. Higher voltage deviations caused by the higher impedance also increase the signal-to-noise ratio of the measurement. The incremental capacity of the battery can also have affected the measurements’ signal-to-noise ratio. The voltage response to an incremental change in the battery’s charge is very small in the region of 50% SOC [47]. In this SOC region, the battery voltage measurements are susceptible to external noise, especially with small current amplitudes [48].

The performance of the OPP can be enhanced by incorporating information on the battery state or by implementing pre-excitations to assess measurement noise. Knowledge of impedance magnitude under different battery states and of noise levels in the measurement setup can be used to adaptively select the harmonics included in the result analysis. For instance, since battery impedance magnitude increases with lower SOC, SOH, and temperature, the energy threshold for including harmonics can be adjusted according to the expected voltage perturbation magnitude [33]. To maintain adequate frequency resolution when modifying the energy threshold, a minimum number of harmonics within frequency ranges of interest should be defined.

The error measure values of the measurement results also show that the OPP signals can be used to measure battery impedance in the low and middle frequency region more accurately than the PRBS. This can be caused by ability of the OPP sequences to suppress second-order non-linearities that affect the battery-impedance at lower frequencies [44]. These regions are important for the analysis of temperature, available power, and SOC [29,49]. Therefore, the using the OPP signals in battery impedance measurements offers high accuracy for analysis of multiple state parameters.

The OPP design method offers a great flexibility to optimize the excitation signal for the used equipment. The OPP M300-signal used in the experiments produced lower error measure values in the high frequency region than the OPP M375 signal or the PRBS. According to (16), the generation frequencies of the OPP signals were 900 Hz and 1,125 Hz for the OPP M300 and OPP M375 respectively. The generation device bandwidth required by the OPP M300 was better suited for the Kepco 50-20MG than the bandwidth required by the other excitations resulting in better measurement accuracy in the high frequency region.

The internal impedance of a Li-ion battery is a crucial parameter for evaluating the battery’s state of health (SOH) and state of charge (SOC). Recent studies have introduced binary and near-binary broadband perturbations that can rapidly and accurately measure battery impedance. However, many of these excitations are designed as discrete-time sequences, and some of their desirable properties are lost when applied to a physical system.

This paper introduced a new method for designing binary excitations for battery-impedance measurements, based on optimized pulse patterns (OPP). The method allows for the design of excitations directly in continuous time, ensuring that the properties of the generated signals remain unchanged when injected into a physical system. Additionally, the method provides the flexibility to freely determine the number of excitation level changes within a single signal period. Signals generated using the OPP method were experimentally compared against a sinusoidal reference measurement and a well-known broadband perturbation, the pseudo-random binary sequence. The analysis of the experimental results demonstrated that signals generated with the proposed OPP method produced impedance measurements with high accuracy and resolution.

Leevi Lignell: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Minh Tran: Writing – review & editing, Software. Tomi Roinila: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
